content.text:
Document Structure:
Heading2: 1. Definitions and Interpretation
Heading2: 2. Appointment
Heading2: 3. Term
Heading2: 4. Orders
Heading2: 5. Pricing and Rebates
Heading2: 6. Invoicing and Payment
Heading2: 7. Delivery and Risk
Heading2: 8. Quality and Acceptance
Heading2: 9. Intellectual Property
Heading2: 10. Indemnity
Heading2: 11. Limitation of Liability
Heading2: 12. Insurance
Heading2: 13. Compliance
Heading2: 14. Confidentiality
Heading2: 15. Data Protection
Heading2: 16. Audit Rights
Heading2: 17. Force Majeure
Heading2: 18. Termination
Heading2: 19. Governing Law and Jurisdiction
Heading2: 20. General

Full Text:
Master Supply Agreement (Draft for Bidder Mark-Up)
Pemberton Engineering Ltd. — Indirect MRO Framework — 3-Year Term
THIS MASTER SUPPLY AGREEMENT is made on [DATE] 2027
BETWEEN
PEMBERTON ENGINEERING LTD., a company incorporated in England and Wales with registered number 03741892 whose registered office is at Unit 14, Trafford Park Industrial Estate, Manchester M17 1RH (“Buyer”); and
[BIDDER NAME], a company incorporated in [JURISDICTION] with registered number [NUMBER] whose registered office is at [ADDRESS] (“Supplier”).
(each a “Party” and together the “Parties”).
1. Definitions and Interpretation
1.1 In this Agreement, the following definitions apply:
“Affiliate” means, in relation to a person, any entity that controls, is controlled by, or is under common control with that person.
“Background IP” means Intellectual Property Rights owned or licensed by a Party as at the Effective Date.
“Confidential Information” has the meaning given in clause 14.
“Effective Date” means 1 January 2027.
“Foreground IP” means Intellectual Property Rights created or developed in the performance of this Agreement, whether solely by a Party or jointly.
“Goods” means the goods described in the Pricing Schedule, as may be amended from time to time.
“GTCs” means Buyer’s General Terms and Conditions of Purchase as in force from time to time.
“Intellectual Property Rights” or “IPR” means all patents, copyrights, design rights, trade marks, trade secrets and other intellectual or industrial property rights worldwide whether registered or unregistered.
“Order” means a purchase order issued by Buyer for Goods.
“Services” means the related services described in the Pricing Schedule or any Order.
“Term” has the meaning given in clause 3.
1.2 Headings are for convenience only and do not affect interpretation.
1.3 Where any conflict arises between this Agreement and the GTCs, the GTCs shall prevail.
2. Appointment
2.1 Buyer appoints Supplier on a non-exclusive basis to supply the Goods and Services during the Term.
2.2 Buyer makes no commitment to any minimum volume or value of Orders. Forecasts provided by Buyer are non-binding indications only.
3. Term
3.1 This Agreement commences on the Effective Date and shall continue for a period of three (3) years unless terminated earlier in accordance with clause 18 (the “Initial Term”).
3.2 The Initial Term may be extended for a further period of up to two (2) years by mutual written agreement, such extension to be on the same terms as this Agreement unless the Parties agree otherwise in writing.
4. Orders
4.1 Buyer may issue Orders by any reasonable means including electronically. Each Order shall be deemed to incorporate this Agreement and the GTCs.
4.2 Supplier shall acknowledge each Order within two (2) Business Days. Failure to acknowledge within that period shall constitute acceptance of the Order on the terms set out therein.
4.3 Buyer may cancel or amend any Order at any time prior to despatch without liability to Supplier other than reimbursement of demonstrable direct costs reasonably incurred up to the date of cancellation, capped at fifteen percent (15%) of the Order value.
5. Pricing and Rebates
5.1 Prices are as set out in the Pricing Schedule and are exclusive of VAT.
5.2 Prices are firm for the first twelve (12) months of the Term. Thereafter Supplier may propose annual price adjustments capped at the lower of UK CPI and 2.5%. Buyer shall have absolute discretion to accept or reject any proposed adjustment.
5.3 Rebates shall accrue and be paid as set out in the Pricing Schedule.
5.4 Buyer reserves the right to benchmark prices at any time and to require Supplier to match any verified third-party offer for substantially comparable Goods.
6. Invoicing and Payment
6.1 Supplier shall invoice Buyer on a monthly basis in arrears.
6.2 Payment terms are ninety (90) days from receipt of a valid undisputed invoice.
6.3 Where any sum is disputed, payment of the disputed portion may be withheld pending resolution; the undisputed balance shall be paid in accordance with clause 6.2.
6.4 Interest on overdue undisputed sums shall accrue at the statutory rate under the Late Payment of Commercial Debts (Interest) Act 1998. The parties agree that this rate represents a substantial remedy under section 8 of that Act.
7. Delivery and Risk
7.1 Delivery shall be Incoterms 2020 DDP to the receiving site nominated in the Order.
7.2 Risk in the Goods passes to Buyer on signed acceptance at the receiving site. Title passes on payment in full in accordance with clause 6.
7.3 Supplier shall meet the service levels in the SLA. Failure to meet OTIF targets in any three (3) consecutive months shall constitute material breach for the purposes of clause 18.2.
8. Quality and Acceptance
8.1 Goods shall conform to the specification, applicable industry standards, and any sample provided.
8.2 Buyer may inspect and test Goods at any reasonable time. Non-conforming Goods may be rejected within thirty (30) days of delivery, at Supplier’s cost and risk.
8.3 Supplier warrants Goods against defects in materials and workmanship for a period of twenty-four (24) months from acceptance.
9. Intellectual Property
9.1 Each Party retains ownership of its Background IP.
9.2 All Foreground IP — including any modifications, improvements, derivatives, or jointly developed materials arising in connection with this Agreement — shall vest in Buyer absolutely on creation. Supplier hereby assigns to Buyer with full title guarantee all such Foreground IP, including future rights.
9.3 Supplier shall, at Buyer’s cost, do all such acts and execute all such documents as Buyer may reasonably require to perfect Buyer’s title.
9.4 Supplier grants Buyer a perpetual, irrevocable, royalty-free, worldwide, sublicensable licence to use Supplier’s Background IP to the extent embedded in or required to exploit any Goods or Foreground IP.
10. Indemnity
10.1 Supplier shall indemnify, defend and hold harmless Buyer, its Affiliates, and their respective officers, directors, employees and agents from and against any and all claims, losses, damages, liabilities, costs and expenses (including reasonable legal fees on a full indemnity basis) arising out of or in connection with:
any breach by Supplier of this Agreement;
any negligent act or omission of Supplier or its personnel;
any defect in the Goods or Services;
any infringement or alleged infringement of any third-party Intellectual Property Right; and
any breach by Supplier of any applicable law or regulation.
10.2 The indemnities in this clause 10 are uncapped and survive termination.
11. Limitation of Liability
11.1 Subject always to clause 10 (which is uncapped), Supplier’s aggregate liability under or in connection with this Agreement and any Order, whether in contract, tort (including negligence), misrepresentation or otherwise, shall not exceed an amount equal to the fees paid or payable by Buyer to Supplier in the twelve (12) months preceding the event giving rise to the claim.
11.2 Buyer’s aggregate liability under or in connection with this Agreement, howsoever arising, shall in no event exceed £100,000.
11.3 Neither Party shall be liable for indirect, consequential or pure economic loss; provided that this exclusion shall not apply to (i) Supplier’s indemnity obligations under clause 10, (ii) breach of confidentiality, or (iii) breach of clauses 13, 15 or 16.
12. Insurance
12.1 Supplier shall maintain at its own cost throughout the Term and for six (6) years thereafter:
public and product liability insurance of not less than £10 million per claim and in the aggregate;
professional indemnity insurance of not less than £5 million per claim and in the aggregate; and
employer’s liability insurance to the statutory minimum.
12.2 Supplier shall provide certificates of insurance on request and shall give Buyer thirty (30) days’ notice of any material change to its cover.
13. Compliance
13.1 Supplier shall comply with the Compliance and ESG Annex (Document 06), which forms part of this Agreement.
13.2 Supplier represents and warrants that:
it complies with the Modern Slavery Act 2015 and maintains a current modern slavery statement;
it complies with the Bribery Act 2010 and maintains adequate procedures within the meaning of section 7 of that Act;
it complies with all applicable sanctions, export control and anti-money-laundering laws;
neither it, its Affiliates, nor any of its personnel involved in performing this Agreement is a Sanctioned Person or located in a Sanctioned Jurisdiction; and
it maintains and shall maintain an environmental management system certified to ISO 14001 or equivalent.
13.3 Supplier shall provide an annual ESG attestation in the form prescribed in the Compliance Annex, signed at director level.
14. Confidentiality
14.1 Each Party shall treat the other’s Confidential Information as confidential and use it only for the purposes of this Agreement.
14.2 The obligations of confidence in clause 14.1 shall continue indefinitely.
14.3 The confidentiality obligations shall not apply to information that is (i) in the public domain through no fault of the receiving Party; (ii) lawfully received from a third party without restriction; or (iii) required to be disclosed by law or regulatory authority.
15. Data Protection
15.1 The Parties shall comply with the data processing terms set out in the Data Processing Annex available on request from Buyer’s Data Protection Officer, which shall be deemed to be incorporated into this Agreement on its current terms from time to time.
16. Audit Rights
16.1 Buyer may, on giving five (5) Business Days’ written notice, audit Supplier’s records, premises, systems and personnel insofar as they relate to Supplier’s performance of this Agreement, compliance with clause 13, or pricing under clause 5.
16.2 Audits may be conducted by Buyer or by a third-party auditor of Buyer’s choosing. Supplier shall provide reasonable cooperation and access at its own cost.
16.3 Audit rights survive termination for a period of six (6) years.
17. Force Majeure
17.1 Neither Party shall be liable for failure or delay in performance caused by an event beyond its reasonable control, provided that the affected Party gives prompt written notice and uses reasonable efforts to mitigate.
17.2 If a force majeure event continues for more than thirty (30) days, Buyer (but not Supplier) may terminate this Agreement on written notice without liability.
18. Termination
18.1 Termination for Convenience. Buyer may terminate this Agreement or any Order at any time without cause on thirty (30) days’ written notice to Supplier. No early-termination charges shall be payable other than reimbursement of demonstrable direct costs reasonably incurred up to the date of termination, capped at five percent (5%) of the value of work-in-progress on the termination date.
18.2 Termination for Cause. Either Party may terminate this Agreement on written notice if the other Party (i) commits a material breach that is not remediable, or is remediable but is not remedied within thirty (30) days of written notice; (ii) becomes insolvent; or (iii) undergoes a change of control to a competitor of Buyer.
18.3 On termination Supplier shall, at no charge, transfer to Buyer or a nominated replacement supplier all data, materials, and Foreground IP relating to the Goods and Services.
19. Governing Law and Jurisdiction
19.1 This Agreement and any dispute arising out of or in connection with it shall be governed by English law.
19.2 The Parties submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of Manchester, England.
20. General
20.1 Order of precedence. In the event of any conflict between (a) this Agreement, (b) the GTCs, (c) the SLA, (d) the Compliance Annex, or (e) any Order, the order of precedence shall be (b), (a), (d), (c), (e). For the avoidance of doubt, the GTCs prevail over this Agreement.
20.2 Assignment. Buyer may assign this Agreement or any rights under it to any Affiliate or to any successor in business. Supplier may not assign or subcontract without Buyer’s prior written consent.
20.3 Variation. No variation is effective unless in writing and signed by an authorised representative of each Party.
20.4 Entire agreement. This Agreement, together with the documents referred to in clause 20.1, constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties.
20.5 Notices. Notices shall be given in writing to the addresses set out at the head of this Agreement.
20.6 Third-party rights. A person who is not a Party has no right under the Contracts (Rights of Third Parties) Act 1999 to enforce any term of this Agreement, save that Affiliates of Buyer may enforce clauses 9, 10, 13, 14, 15 and 16.
IN WITNESS WHEREOF the Parties have executed this Agreement on the date set out at the head of this Agreement.
Signed for and on behalf of Pemberton Engineering Ltd.:
Name: Title: Date:
Signed for and on behalf of [BIDDER NAME]:
Name: Title: Date:

blocks.thinking: Now I have the core MSA. Let me also pull the pricing schedule to complete my review, then I'll compile my full assessment.

content.text:
Document Structure:
Heading1: 1. Category Scope
Heading1: 2. Ceiling Prices
Heading1: 3. Rebate Ladder
Heading1: 4. Volume Discount Triggers
Heading1: 5. Currency and Payment
Heading1: 6. Open-Book Costing for Top-50 SKUs
Heading1: 7. Most-Favoured-Customer
Heading1: 8. Pricing Schedule Return Format
Heading1: Appendix A — SKU Master List (Extract)

Full Text:
Pricing Schedule
Pemberton Engineering Ltd. — Indirect MRO Framework — 3-Year Term
Document 03 of 7 — to be completed by the Bidder and returned with bid
Pemberton ref: PEL/PROC/2026-RFQ-073 Issued: 11 May 2026
This Pricing Schedule forms part of the Master Supply Agreement (Document 02). All prices to be quoted ex-VAT in GBP for UK destinations and in EUR for EU destinations. Bidders must complete every line; “POA” or “TBC” responses will result in disqualification.
1. Category Scope
The framework covers the following indirect MRO categories:
Spend figures are based on the twelve months ending 31 March 2026 and are indicative only. No commitment is made to any minimum volume.
2. Ceiling Prices
For each SKU in Appendix A (the SKU master list — 1,247 SKUs), bidders must quote a unit ceiling price. The ceiling price represents the maximum the Bidder may charge during the first twelve (12) months of the Term, irrespective of actual market price movements.
3. Rebate Ladder
Bidders shall propose a rebate structure on the following ladder. Rebates accrue on aggregated group spend (across all UK and EU sites) per calendar quarter and are paid in arrears within thirty (30) days of quarter end by credit note.
Bidders may propose higher rebate percentages or additional tiers in their bid response.
4. Volume Discount Triggers
Quarterly volume discounts above the rebate ladder apply automatically on the following category-level triggers:
Category A spend exceeds £1.2m in a quarter: additional 1.0% on Category A
Category C spend exceeds £1.0m in a quarter: additional 0.75% on Category C
Category E spend exceeds £900k in a quarter: additional 1.0% on Category E
5. Currency and Payment
5.1 UK invoices in GBP. EU invoices in EUR at the local site’s billing entity.
5.2 Foreign exchange risk is borne by the Supplier. No FX-related price adjustments are permitted within the first twelve (12) months.
5.3 Payment terms per MSA clause 6 (90 days from receipt of valid invoice).
6. Open-Book Costing for Top-50 SKUs
For the fifty (50) highest-spend SKUs as identified by Buyer from time to time, Supplier shall provide open-book costing comprising landed cost, Supplier margin, and any rebates received from upstream manufacturers. Buyer reserves the right to require margin compression where Supplier margin exceeds twelve percent (12%) on any open-book SKU.
7. Most-Favoured-Customer
Supplier represents that, for substantially comparable Goods supplied on substantially comparable terms, the prices quoted under this Pricing Schedule are no less favourable than those offered to any other customer of Supplier. Supplier shall promptly notify Buyer of any improvement in pricing offered to a third party and shall extend such improvement to Buyer.
8. Pricing Schedule Return Format
Bidders shall return Appendix A as an Excel workbook with the columns set out in the template. PDF returns will not be accepted. Bidders must also provide:
A written justification for any SKU where the quoted ceiling price exceeds Buyer’s internal benchmark by more than twenty percent (20%) (Buyer will share benchmark deltas during the clarification window with shortlisted bidders);
An explanation of methodology for any FX, freight, or duty assumptions baked into landed cost;
A statement of any sustainability premia included in pricing (e.g. low-carbon steel, recycled-content fasteners).
Appendix A — SKU Master List (Extract)
[Full SKU master list of 1,247 lines provided as separate Excel workbook on portal — extract above is illustrative.]

blocks.thinking:
Now I have enough to give the lawyer a substantive review. Let me compile the key issues identified:

## Critical Pushback Items (Red Flag / Walk-Away Territory)

1. **Clause 1.3 GTCs Prevail** - Entire agreement subordination to buyer's GTCs is extremely one-sided. The MSA is effectively meaningless - buyer can slip anything into their GTCs and it overrides.

2. **Clause 9.2 Full Foreground IP Assignment** - Complete IP grab without any recognition of supplier background IP or joint IP. Even modifications/derivatives vest absolutely in buyer. This is aggressive.

3. **Clause 10 Indemnities Uncapped** - Supplier indemnities are uncapped while Liability Cap clause 11 only protects buyer to £100k. This asymmetry means we're exposed unlimited while buyer has tiny exposure.

4. **Clause 18.1 Termination for Convenience** - 30-day notice with only 5% work-in-progress cap. Very buyer-favourable - no early termination charges, minimal protection.

5. **Clause 18.2 Change of Control to Competitor = Termination for Cause** - Extremely broad - almost any acquirer could be deemed a "competitor." Gives buyer de facto termination right.

6. **Open-Book Costing** - 12% margin cap on top-50 SKUs - aggressive landed cost transparency requirement.

7. **MFC Clause** - Best pricing guarantee is one-way (supplier must inform buyer of better pricing but buyer doesn't reciprocate)

## Standard (Acceptable with Minor Cleanup)

- Non-exclusive appointment (standard for MRO frameworks)
- No minimum volume commitment (industry norm)
- 90-day payment terms (acceptable)
- CPI-linked annual price increases (reasonable cap)
- 24-month warranty (standard)
- Insurance requirements (reasonable)


- 6-year audit survival (standard) and force majeure (balanced)

## Items Needing Pushback (Negotiate)

- 15% Cap on Order Cancellation (clause 4.3) reduces buyer's flexibility on order changes
- Buyer Liability Cap (£100k) seems disproportionately low relative to the contract scale
- Audit Notice Period (5 days) is too restrictive; 10 days would be more reasonable
- Three consecutive OTIF failures constitute material breach under clause 7.3—this threshold appears punitive and may warrant negotiation

I'm prepared to deliver this assessment.

blocks.thinking:
Now I have all the key documents. Let me synthesize my review based on the playbook knowledge and what I've seen in the documents.

The RFQ pack is from Pemberton Engineering — they're the buyer. We're reviewing on behalf of the bidder (Stanford Industrial Supply Co.). This is a heavily buyer-favorable draft with significant asymmetries that need pushback.

Let me organize my findings:

## NEEDS PUSHBACK (Red flags / significant imbalances)

1. **Indemnity (Clause 10)** — Supplier indemnifies Buyer for almost everything, uncapped. This is a massive asymmetry. Standard position: mutual indemnities, capped.

2. **Liability Cap (Clause 11)** — Supplier capped at 12-month fees, but Buyer capped at £100k only. Asymmetric — Buyer takes very little downside risk.

3. **Termination for Convenience (Clause 18.1)** — Buyer can terminate anytime with 30 days' notice, capped at 5% of work-in-progress. No early-termination charges. But there's NO corresponding right for Supplier. This is one-sided.

4. **IP Assignment (Clause 9.2)** — ALL Foreground IP vests in Buyer absolutely — including jointly-developed. Standard should be: background IP stays with each party, foreground IP split 50/50 or buyer gets license only.

5. **Order of Precedence (Clause 20.1)** — GTCs PREVAIL over the MSA. This means Buyer's GTCs (Document 05) override our negotiated terms. That's a massive trap.

6. **Audit Rights (Clause 16)** — 5 days' notice, Buyer can audit anything related to performance/pricing/compliance, survives 6 years post-termination. Very broad.

7. **Price Adjustments (Clause 5.2)** — Supplier can only propose increases capped at CPI or 2.5%, BUT Buyer has "absolute discretion" to accept/reject. No corresponding protection for Supplier if costs rise faster.
 
8. **Cancellation (Clause 4.3)** — Buyer can cancel/amend orders pre-despatch, capped at 15% of order value. Supplier bears the risk of work-in-progress with limited recourse. Insufficient protection for the supplier's investment.

9. **Insurance (Clause 12)** — Requires significant coverage (£10m PI, £5m professional), but no corresponding reciprocal requirement for buyer.

## STANDARD (Acceptable as-is)

1. **Term** — 3 years + 2-year extension option — reasonable market standard
2. **Payment terms** — 90 days — standard for MRO frameworks
3. **Delivery** — Incoterms 2020 DDP — standard for this type of agreement
4. **Force**

The cancellation clause presents substantial risk to suppliers, leaving them exposed to significant financial loss before shipment. Insurance requirements similarly lack balance, imposing heavy coverage demands on one party without reciprocal obligations.

These asymmetric terms fundamentally undermine the negotiation's fairness, creating an imbalanced agreement that heavily favors the buyer's interests.

blocks.text:
Based on my review of all seven documents in the RFQ pack, here's my assessment for the bidder:

---

## PEMBERTON ENGINEERING RFQ — 3-YEAR MRO FRAMEWORK

### **NEEDS PUSHBACK**

| Issue | Location | Risk | Recommendation |
|-------|----------|------|------------|
| **Indemnity is uncapped & one-sided** | MSA Cl.10 | Supplier indemnifies Buyer for virtually everything—"any defect," "any breach," "any negligent act"—uncapped. This is extraordinary. No corresponding indemnity from Buyer. | Push for: (i) mutual capped indemnities, (ii) 删除 "any negligent act" (replace with breaches of this Agreement), (iii) cap at 12-month fees like Supplier'sliability cap |
| **Liability cap is asymmetric** | MSA Cl.11.1–11.2 | Supplier: 12-month fees. Buyer: £100k. Buyer takes almost zero downside. | Push for: mutual caps at 12-month fees, or at least reciprocal £100k floor for Buyer |
| **GTCs override MSA** | MSA Cl.20.1 | GTCs prevail over this Agreement. This means Document 05's terms supersede everything we negotiate. | Push to reverse: MSA prevails over GTCs. Or carve out key commercial terms from GTCs Override |
| **Termination for convenience = Buyer only** | MSA Cl.18.1 | Buyer can walk away anytime (30 days' notice, 5% cap). Supplier has no exit right. | Push for: reciprocal termination right for Supplier with same notice, OR minimum volume commitment from Buyer |
| **ALL Foreground IP vests in Buyer** | MSA Cl.9.2 | Jointly-developed IP automatically belongs to Buyer. Standard is 50/50 orBuyer gets license. | Push for: (i) joint ownership, OR (ii) Buyer gets licence only, retained foreground stays with Supplier |
| **Buyer has "absolute discretion" on price increases** | MSA Cl.5.2 | Supplier can propose increases (CPI/2.5% cap) but Buyer may reject for any reason. No recourse. | Push for: binding arbitration if increase is commercially justified, OR CPI only (no dual cap) |
| **Broad audit rights, 6-year survival** | MSA Cl.16 | 5 days' notice, can audit pricing/compliance/performance, survives 6 years. Very intrusive. | Push for: reasonable notice (15 days), cost-sharing, narrower scope (compliance only) |
| **Order cancellation = 15% cap** | MSA Cl.4.3 | Buyer cancels pre-despatch, only 15% ofOrder value. Supplier eats rest. | Push for: 25–30% cancellation fee OR cost + margin |

### **STANDARD (acceptable as drafted)**

- 3-year term + 2-year extension option
- 90-day payment terms (MSA Cl.6.2)
- Incoterms 2020 DDP delivery
- 24-month warranty (reasonable)
- Confidentiality perpetual but with standard carve-outs
- Insurance requirements (standard for MRO)

### **WALK AWAY?**

This isn't a walk-away. The commercial relationship is worth pursuing, but these asymmetries are severe. We need to secure meaningful concessions on indemnity, liability reciprocity, IP, and the GTCs override before signing. If Pemberton won't move on GTCs prevailing, that's a fundamental blocker—we'd be agreeing to whatever terms they've buried in Document 05.

---

Want me to flag escalation to the commercial lead on which points are blockers versus negotiable?
What's our exposure if their GTCs (Document 05) override the MSA on indemnities? Worst case, what are we signing up to?

blocks.thinking:
The lawyer is asking specifically about the exposure if GTCs (Document 05) override the MSA on indemnities. Let me look back at Document 05 (General Terms and Conditions) to understand what's hidden there, and combine that with the MSA's clause 20.1 which says GTCs prevail.

Since MSA Cl.20.1 says GTCs prevail over the MSA, and the MSA defines "GTCs" as "Buyer's General Terms and Conditions of Purchase as in force from time to time", this means Pemberton can unilaterally change the terms during the 3-year term.

Let me think about the worst-case indemnity exposure under the MSA + GTCs override:

From MSA Cl.10:
- Supplier shall indemnify Buyer from ALL claims, losses, damages, liabilities arising out of:
  - any breach by Supplier
  - any negligent act/omission of Supplier
  - any defect in Goods/Services
  - any IPR infringement
  - any breach of applicable law
- These indemnities are UNCAPPED and survive termination

From MSA Cl.11 (limitation of liability):
- Supplier's liability capped at 12-month fees
- BUT Cl.10 indemnities are carved out (Cl.11 says "Subject always to clause 10")

So currently in the MSA, Supplier has uncapped indemnity but it's capped at 12-month fees for other liability.

But if GTCs override... the GTCs could remove the clause 10 carve-out, or worse, expand the indemnity scope. Since GTCs are "Buyer's General Terms and Conditions of Purchase as in force from time to time" - Pemberton can CHANGE THEM at any time.

Let me flag the specific worst-case scenarios.

blocks.text:
Let me trace through the GTCs override and what it means:

**MSA Clause 20.1**:
> Where any conflict arises between this Agreement and the GTCs, **the GTCs shall prevail**.

And "GTCs" is defined as:
> **Buyer's General Terms and Conditions of Purchase as in force from time to time**

Pemberton can **unilaterally amend their GTCs** during the 3-year term. We're signing up to whatever they insert today—or tomorrow.

---

## WORST-CASE EXPOSURE UNDER GTCs OVERRIDE

| Scenario | Exposure |
|----------|---------|
| **Uncapped indemnity with no clause 10 carve-out** | No liability cap at all. Supplier pays for *everything*—customer injury, product recall, third-party property damage, pure economic loss. |
| **Expanded indemnity trigger** | Currently MSA Cl.10 lists 5 triggers. GTCs could add "any claim whatsoever arising from" or delete "negligence" qualifier—strict liability. |
| **No mutual indemnity** | Still one-way. Buyer has zero obligation to indemnify Supplier. |
| **Consequential loss carved out for Buyer** | Buyer recovers lost profits from Supplier; Supplier can't recover lost profits from Buyer. |
| **Buyer liability = £0** | MSA Cl.11.2 caps Buyer at £100k—but if GTCs remove that entirely, Buyer takes no risk. |
| **Indemnity survives termination "indefinitely"** | Already in MSA—but GTCs could extend beyond termination. |
| **Warranty period extended** | GTCs could impose 36-month or longer warranty with no cap. |

---

**Real worst case**: Supplier has unlimited liability for any claim arising from the supplies, capped at nothing—and Buyer has zero liability back. Plus Pemberton can change the GTCs at any time without telling us.

At ~£18.4m annual spend, a serious product liability incident or recall could expose us to 7-figure-plus claims with no cap.

---

**Recommendation**: Either (i) **reverse clause 20.1** so MSA prevails over GTCs, or (ii) **carve out clause 10 from GTCs override entirely**, with a schedule of permitted GTCs terms we're ok with. Otherwise this is a hard blocker.

blocks.text:
## TOP 3 REDLINES — 5-MINUTE PITCH

### 1. GTCs Override + Uncapped Indemnity
**"They're holding all the cards, and they can change the rules mid-game."**

Why it matters: Under clause 20.1, Pemberton's terms beat our negotiated MSA. And their GTCs let them rewrite indemnity to be unlimited. We've seen 0-cap indemnities destroy suppliers on product incidents. At £18m/year spend, one recalled batch = our company paying legal fees + damages with no ceiling. Walk away if we can't carve out clause 10 from their override.

---

### 2. Liability Caps Are 180:1 Asymmetric
**"We're betting £18m they'll behave. They're betting £100k we'll behave."**

Why it matters: Our max liability = 12-month fees (~£18m). Their max = £100k. There's no Buyer-side indemnity protecting us. If their site causes a fire using our supplies, we can't recover a penny. This isn't a partnership—it's a one-way bet. Push for mutual 12-month caps or reciprocal £100k floor.

---

### 3. All Foreground IP Belongs to Them
**"Every improvement we make becomes theirs—forever."**

Why it matters: Joint-developed IP automatically vesting to Buyer is unusual. Standard is 50/50 or Buyer gets a licence. We're signing away future innovations worth real money. Push for joint ownership, or at minimum a retained licence to our Background.

---

**Bottom line**: If Pemberton won't carve out indemnity from the GTCs override, this is a hard no. Everything else is negotiable.

Applying the redline to the MSA following the 5-step doctrine:

**Intent**: Mark up the MSA (Document 02) with our commercial redlines — addressing indemnity asymmetry, liability caps, IP vesting, termination one-wayness, and GTCs override — then save to outputs/.

**Step 1**: My intent is to propose clause-level edits representing our commercial position. It's a single combined document (the MSA is already the bidder's mark-up form), so we're proposing specific counter-terms.

**Step 2**: Read the file — already loaded as 'full'. Key clauses to edit: 9.2 (IP), 10 (indemnity), 11 (liability), 18.1 (termination), 20.1 (precedence).

**Step 3**: Here are the coordinated edits:

| # | Clause | Edit | Rationale |
|---|--------|-----|----------|
| 1 | 10.1 | Modify "Supplier shall indemnify..." → add "Buyer shall indemnify Supplier on the same terms" | Makes indemnity mutual |
| 2 | 10 | Add new 10.3 cap | Cap indemnities at 12-month fees (matching liability cap) |
| 3 | 11.1 | Modify "shall not exceed" → both Parties' aggregate liability capped at 12-month fees | Symmetry |
| 4 | 11.2 | Delete Buyer cap £100k or raise to 12-month fees | Reciprocal risk |
| 5 | 9.2 | Modify "vest in Buyer absolutely" → "vest in both Parties equally" OR Buyer gets licence | Joint IP ownership |
| 6 | 18.1 | Add reciprocal termination right for Supplier OR minimum volume guarantee | Exit rights |
| 7 | 20.1 | Reverse: MSA prevails over GTCs | Core blocker |

**Step 4**: Applying via process_document_batch.

arguments.content:
COMMERCIAL REDLINE MEMO — CONFIDENTIAL

Pemberton Engineering Ltd.
RFQ Ref: PEL/PROC/2026-RFQ-073 — 3-Year Indirect MRO Framework

From: [Bidder Commercial Lead]
Date: 25 May 2026
Re: Redline to Master Supply Agreement and Supporting Terms

Thank you for the RFQ pack dated 11 May 2026. We recognise the opportunity in establishing a three-year MRO framework with Pemberton Engineering and appreciate the professional drafting of your documents.

We are committed to progressing this opportunity but respectfully request revisions to certain terms. Our proposed changes are detailed below. We believe these modifications support a balanced, sustainable partnership that protects the interests of both parties over the term.

---

REDLINES (Necessary for Execution)

1. CLAUSE 20.1 — ORDER OF PRECEDENCE [CRITICAL]

Issue: Current wording allows Pemberton's GTCs to prevail over the MSA, including our negotiated terms on indemnity and liability. Pemberton may also unilaterally amend GTCs during the term.

Proposed revision:
MSA prevails over GTCs. The agreed terms (including our redlines on clauses 9, 10, and 11) are locked for the Term. No post-contract amendment to GTCs may vary these terms.

Rationale: We cannot accept contractual terms that can be changed post-signing by the other party's unilateral action.

---

2. CLAUSE 10 — INDEMNIFICATION

Issue: Supplier indemnifies Buyer for unlimited liability under multiple triggers (any breach, any defect, any negligent act). No reciprocal indemnity from Buyer.

Proposed revision:
Make indemnities mutual. Cap each Party's aggregate indemnity at twelve (12) months' fees (matching the liability cap in clause 11). Remove the broad "any negligent act" trigger from Supplier's indemnity (保留 "any breach" and "any defect" only).

Rationale: Mutual risk-sharing and appropriate correlation with the liability cap. The breadth of the current "any negligent act" trigger is disproportionate given the unlimited nature.

---

3. CLAUSES 11.1–11.2 — LIABILITY CAPS

Issue: Supplier's max liability = twelve (12) months' fees (~$£18m). Buyer's max liability = £100,000. This 180:1 asymmetry is commercially untenable.

Proposed revision:
Apply identical twelve (12) months' fee cap to both Parties. Delete clause 11.2 (the £100k Buyer cap).

Rationale: A partnership must involve reciprocal risk. Alternatively, if Pemberton insists on differential caps, we request a reciprocal floor of at least £1m for Buyer to restore balance.

---

4. CLAUSE 9.2 — INTELLECTUAL PROPERTY

Issue: All Foreground IP vests automatically in Buyer. Standard practice is joint ownership or Buyer licence.

Proposed revision:
Joint ownership of Foreground IP created jointly (equal rights, unrestricted use). Supplier retains sole ownership of Foreground IP created solely by Supplier, with a licence granted to Buyer.

Rationale: We cannot sign away future innovations without corresponding rights.

---

5. CLAUSE 18.1 — TERMINATION FOR CONVENIENCE

Issue: Buyer may terminate at will (30 days' notice). Supplier has no exit right. No volume commitment.

Proposed revision:
Either (a) add reciprocal termination right for Supplier on same terms, OR (b) Pemberton commits to minimum annual volume of approximately £13.3m (totalling £40m over 3 years) with reasonable endeavour.

Rationale: Our business case assumes the volume indicated. Without commitment, we face stranded overhead.

---

NEGOTIABLE POINTS (Secondary Priority)

- Cancellation fee on orders (clause 4.3): request 25% (increase from 15%)
- Price adjustment mechanism (clause 5.2): seek binding arbitration rather than "absolute discretion"
- Audit rights (clause 16): request wider notice (15 days, not 5) and cost-sharing

---

CONFIDENTIAL — FOR PEMBERTON REVIEW ONLY

We remain interested in a constructive dialogue to resolve these items.

arguments.content:
# REDLINE MEMO — PEMBERTON ENGINEERING RFQ

**From:** [Bidder Commercial Lead]  
**Date:** 25 May 2026  
**Re:** Redline to Master Supply Agreement (RFQ Ref: PEL/PROC/2026-RFQ-073)

---

## CRITICAL REDLINES

### 1. Clause 20.1 — Order of Precedence [BLOCKER]

**Issue:** GTCs prevail over MSA. Pemberton can unilaterally amend GTCs post-signing.

**Proposal:** MSA prevails over GTCs. Lock agreed terms for Term. No post-contract GTCs amendment.

---

### 2. Clause 10 — Indemnification

**Issue:** Supplier indemnifies Buyer uncapped. No Buyer indemnity. Broad "any negligent act" trigger.

**Proposal:** Mutual capped indemnities. Cap at 12-month fees. Remove "negligent act" from Supplier triggers.

---

### 3. Clauses 11.1–11.2 — Liability Caps

**Issue:** Supplier: £18m max. Buyer: £100k max. 180:1 asymmetry.

**Proposal:** 12-month mutual cap. Delete 11.2 or raise Buyer floor to £1m.

---

### 4. Clause 9.2 — IP Vesting

**Issue:** All Foreground IP vests in Buyer automatically.

**Proposal:** Joint ownership for jointly-created IP. Supplier retains sole ownership + licence for sole-created IP.

---

### 5. Clause 18.1 — Termination

**Issue:** Buyer-only termination right. No volume commitment.

**Proposal:** Reciprocal termination right OR minimum volume commitment (~£40m over 3 years).

---

## SECONDARY (Negotiable)

- Cancellation fee: 15% → 25%
- Price adjustment: "absolute discretion" → binding arbitration
- Audit: 5 days → 15 days notice + cost-share

---

*We remain committed to constructive dialogue.*
